principles: Minimize distractions by reducing visual clutter and non-essential UI elements
Break tasks into small, manageable chunks with clear progress indicators
Provide immediate and clear feedback for every user action
Use time-based cues (timers, progress bars) to support task persistence
Avoid autoplay animations or sudden UI changes that hijack attention
Support single-focus mode to reduce working memory demands
Use progressive disclosure to show only relevant information at each step
cognitive_basis: ADHD affects executive function, working memory, and sustained attention. The prefrontal cortex, responsible for planning and impulse control, shows reduced activity. UI should externalize cognitive scaffolding — providing structure, reminders, and feedback that compensate for difficulties in self-regulation and task management.
design_patterns: Clear visual hierarchy with prominent CTAs
Undo support instead of confirmation dialogs

principles: Use consistent and predictable layouts across all pages
Provide clear, explicit labels and avoid ambiguous icons or metaphors
Minimize sensory overload from bright colors, complex animations, or background noise
Support routine by maintaining stable navigation patterns
Offer explicit state indicators rather than relying on implicit cues
Avoid figurative language or unclear instructions in UI copy
Provide advance notice of changes or transitions
cognitive_basis: Autism involves differences in predictive processing and sensory integration. The brain's ability to form and update predictive models may work differently, making unexpected changes particularly disruptive. Sensory processing differences mean stimuli that are neutral for neurotypical users may be overwhelming. UI should provide explicit, consistent structure that supports pattern recognition.
design_patterns: Explicit state labels (not just color changes)
Transition warnings before major UI changes

principles: Use readable fonts (sans-serif, minimum 16px, adequate letter and word spacing)
Provide sufficient line height (1.5+) and paragraph spacing
Avoid justified text alignment — use left-aligned text
Support text-to-speech and alternative content formats
Use visual aids (icons, images, color coding) alongside text content
Keep sentences short and use simple vocabulary in UI copy
Allow users to customize font size and spacing
cognitive_basis: Dyslexia affects phonological processing — the ability to map written symbols to sounds. This is not a visual problem but a language processing difference. Reading demands significant cognitive resources, leading to faster fatigue. UI should reduce reading demands and provide multimodal information channels (visual + auditory + spatial).
design_patterns: Large, well-spaced text with sans-serif fonts

principles: Provide controls to reduce motion, animations, and transitions
Respect prefers-reduced-motion OS setting automatically
Avoid flashing content (3 flashes per second threshold for epilepsy safety)
Allow users to adjust color intensity and contrast
Minimize auditory stimuli and provide volume controls
Support dark mode and muted color schemes
cognitive_basis: Sensory processing sensitivity affects how the nervous system receives and responds to stimuli. Individuals may experience sensory overload from stimuli that others filter out automatically. This can include visual motion, bright colors, sudden sounds, or high-contrast patterns. UI should provide maximum user control over sensory input.
design_patterns: Quiet default states with optional enhancements

principles: Ensure all interactive elements are keyboard accessible
Provide large click/tap targets (minimum 44x44px)
Support keyboard shortcuts for common actions
Avoid interactions requiring precise mouse movements
Allow sufficient time for user responses — no aggressive timeouts
cognitive_basis: Motor impairments affect the ability to perform precise physical movements. This includes fine motor control needed for mouse targeting and fast keyboard input. UI should minimize the physical demands of interaction by providing multiple input modalities and generous target sizes.
